coredata.dc:description: Ethylene thiourea (ETU) and propylene thiourea (PTU), the degradation products of dithiocarbamates, are carcinogenic, teratogenic, and mutagenic. In this study, we developed an ultrasensitive method for the simultaneous detection of ETU and PTU using amidated carbon quantum dots (A-CDs) as chemiluminescent probes. The distinctive chemiluminescence (CL) mechanisms of the A-CDs were elucidated using CL spectroscopy, radical trapping experiments, and mass spectrometry. ETU or PTU are oxidized by Ce(IV) resulting in the formation of sulfur-based free radicals. Then the radicals are selectively recognized by the A-CDs accompanying with CL emission. The high-performance liquid chromatography-CL method was validated by quantifying ETU and PTU in vegetables, wherein the detection limit was 0.17 μg·kg−1. Comparing with previous literatures, the proposed method demonstrated advantages with lower solvent use, shorter detection time, reduced matrix effects, higher accuracy and precision. The results indicated that ETU was really detected in tomatoes, implying the potential risk of public health.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the HPLC- CL detection system to quantify ethylene thiourea (ETU) and propylene thiourea (PTU). Abbreviations: P1, quaternary pump; P2, peristaltic pump; V, autosampler; F, flow cell; PMT, photomultiplier tube; HV, negative high-voltage supply (700 V); and PC, personal computer.

Effect of (a) synthesis method, (b) mass ratio of CA to l-Ser, (c) microwave power, (d) EDA volume, and (e) microwave duration. HPLC parameters: mobile phase: 7 % MeOH; flow rate: 1.0 mL·min−1; column temperature: 35 °C; injection volume: 100 μL. CL parameters: 1/2000 dilution of CDs, 0.5 mM Ce(IV) (containing 0.01 M H2SO4 and 0.05 M HCl), ETU and PTU (100 μg·L−1 each); flow rate: 2.5 mL·min−1; PMT voltage: 800 V.

Transmission electron micrographs (a), XRD patterns (b), FT-IR spectra (c), XPS survey (d) and high-resolution C1s (e), N1s (f), O1s spectra (g), Raman spectra (h), and UV–Vis excitation spectra and FL emission spectra (i) of an aqueous solution of A-CDs. Inset of (i): Photograph of A-CDs in a well-lit room (left) and in a dark room under UV-light irradiation (365 nm, right). (j) FL spectra excited at wavelengths of 300–430 nm in 10 nm increments.

(a) CL spectrum of A-CDs–Ce(IV) system (black line), A-CDs–ETU–Ce(IV) system (green line), A-CDs–PTU–Ce(IV) system (blue line), PL of A-CDs (λex = 435 nm, purple line), and (e) PL spectra of Ox-A-CDs after the reaction (λex = 435 nm, red line). Conditions: 0.4 mM Ce(IV) in 0.02 M H2SO4 and 0.04 M HCl, 1:2500 diluted A-CDs, 100 μg/L of ETU and PTU, flow rate: 4.0 mL·min−1. (b) UV–Vis spectra of PTU (line a); A-CDs (line b); a mixture of A-CDs, PTU, and Ce(IV) (line c); a mixture of A-CDs and Ce(IV) (line d); a mixture of PTU and Ce(IV) (line e); and Ce(IV) (line f). Conditions: PTU: 1 mg‧L−1, A-CDs: 1/1000 dilution (∼2 mg‧L−1), Ce(IV): 0.25 mM in 0.01 M H2SO4 and 0.02 M HCl. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Plausible mechanism for the ETU- or PTU-catalyzed enhancement of the A-CDs–Ce(IV) CL system.

Chromatographs of (a) tomato and spiked tomato; and (b) sweet potato and spiked sweet potato. Conditions: HPLC parameters: mobile phase, 4 % MeOH; flow rate, 1.0 mL·min−1; column oven, 35 °C; injection volume, 100 μL; and spiked ETU and PTU, 20 μg·kg−1. CL parameters: dilution ratio of A-CDs, 1/2500; 0.4 mM Ce(IV) (containing 0.008 M H2SO4 and 0.05 M HCl); flow rate, 3.5 mL·min−1; and PMT voltage, 700 V.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ethylene thiourea (ETU) and propylene thiourea (PTU), the degradation products of dithiocarbamates, are carcinogenic, teratogenic, and mutagenic. In this study, we developed an ultrasensitive method for the simultaneous detection of ETU and PTU using amidated carbon quantum dots (A-CDs) as chemiluminescent probes. The distinctive chemiluminescence (CL) mechanisms of the A-CDs were elucidated using CL spectroscopy, radical trapping experiments, and mass spectrometry. ETU or PTU are oxidized by Ce(IV) resulting in the formation of sulfur-based free radicals. Then the radicals are selectively recognized by the A-CDs accompanying with CL emission. The high-performance liquid chromatography-CL method was validated by quantifying ETU and PTU in vegetables, wherein the detection limit was 0.17 μg·kg−1. Comparing with previous literatures, the proposed method demonstrated advantages with lower solvent use, shorter detection time, reduced matrix effects, higher accuracy and precision. The results indicated that ETU was really detected in tomatoes, implying the potential risk of public health.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the 1940s, dithiocarbamates (DTCs) have been one of the most commonly used fungicides(Fanjul-Bolado et al., 2021) applied to over 70 types of crops, such as potatoes, tomatoes, grapes, and oranges, to protect against approximately 400 pathogens. DTCs are categorized into three groups based on their chemical structure: propylenedithiocarbamates (PBDCs), such as propineb; ethylenedithiocarbamates (EBDCs), including mancozeb; and dimethyl dithiocarbamates, including thiram, ziram, and other related compounds(Veiga-del-Baño et al., 2023). Unfortunately, they are extremely unstable. For instance, in the presence of water and/or oxygen, they degrade rapidly to produce highly polar compounds such as ethylene thiourea (ETU) and propylene thiourea (PTU)(Stadler et al., 2022). ETU and PTU are the biological metabolites and environmental degradation products of EBDCs(Mandic-Rajcevic et al., 2019) and PBDCs(Song et al., 2018), respectively. However, ETU and PTU are more toxic than their parent compounds(Chung & Wong, 2022). Notably, ETU was listed as a “possibly carcinogenic substance to humans (2B)” by the International Agency for Research on Cancer in 1974. Similarly, structured PTU was more carcinogenic to animals than ETU(López-Fernández et al., 2014; Ripollés et al., 2012).

Over the past two decades, 25,000–35,000 tons of DTCs have been used annually worldwide(Campanale et al., 2023; Martins & Melchert, 2023), presenting safety concerns. People are primarily being exposed to ETU and PTU through their diet(Maranghi et al., 2013). Therefore, their concentrations in food must be determined. However, as their concentration levels in food are extremely low (usually trace or ultra-trace levels), ultrasensitive analytical methods must be developed for this purpose. Thus far, ETU and PTU concentrations in food have been determined via a ratiometric fluorescence (FL) method based on sulfur quantum dots stabilized on Ag clusters(Han et al., 2023), a FL method based on Ce(IV) oxidation kinetics(Pérez-Ruiz et al., 2001), cathodic stripping voltammetry(de Carvalho et al., 2004), and ELISA(Aly et al., 2020). However, these methods are either significantly affected by complex matrices due to inadequate selectivity or cannot simultaneously quantify ETU and PTU. The primary analytical methods applied to the simultaneous determination of ETU and PTU are GC–MS(López-Fernández et al., 2014) and LC-MS/MS(Bendhiab et al., 2023; Vaclavik et al., 2018). GC–MS necessitates analyte derivatization, which often leads to inconsistent recoveries, reduced sensitivity, and additional labor-intensive steps. In contrast, LC-MS/MS is highly sensitive and selective. However, this method requires intricate purification to prevent the pretreatment reagents from compromising the mass spectrum; this method is also expensive, which limits broader application. In addition, HPLC-UV(Wei et al., 2016) and HPLC-DAD(López-Fernández et al., 2014) methods are not sufficiently sensitive for detecting ETU and PTU concentrations in food because of severe matrix interference and complicated purification and enrichment steps. UHPLC-electrochemistry (ECD)(Nantaphol et al., 2020) has also been used to detect ETU and PTU in food. However, electrode fouling and insensitivity limit the applications of this method. Hence, a more selective, sensitive, and practical method for detecting ETU and PTU in food must be developed.

Chemiluminescence (CL)-based detection methods usually have road linear ranges, high detection speed, and low detection limits(Timofeeva et al., 2018; Zhao, Ma, et al., 2023). These methods have been widely used in food(Shah et al., 2023), medicine(Iranifam, 2013), environment(Zhu et al., 2023), and biological analysis(Zhao, Xu, et al., 2023). In recent years, the CL of nanomaterials such as covalent organic frameworks(Guo et al., 2024), metal–organic frameworks(Wang et al., 2023; C. Zhao, Ma, et al., 2023), black phosphorus quantum dots(Jia et al., 2020), polymer dots(Haghighi Shishavan & Amjadi, 2021), carbon dots (CDs)(Hao et al., 2024), MoS2 quantum dots(Dou et al., 2019), double-layer hydroxides(Pan et al., 2018), and metal nanomaterials(Song et al., 2024) has been studied. However, metal-based nanomaterials often face challenges including potential cytotoxicity, bioaccumulation concerns in biological systems, and environmental persistence raising ecological risks. CD-based methods have become popular owing to their abundant supply, straightforward and diverse synthesis methods, and excellent biocompatibility and photostability(Azimi et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022). However, to the best of our knowledge, CL methods have not been used to simultaneously detect ETU and PTU.

In this study, we fabricated amidated CDs (A-CDs) with ethylenediamine (EDA) as additives to sensitively detect ETU and PTU with acidic Ce(IV) as an oxidant and characterized them through Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, FL spectroscopy, ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Raman spectroscopy, and X-ray diffraction (XRD). We also investigated the CL mechanisms using FL spectroscopy, free radical capture, and mass spectrometry, and established a novel HPLC-CL method based on A-CDs as probes for detecting ETU and PTU in vegetables.

Ultrapure water (resistance: 18.2 MΩ cm) from a Milli-Q system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) was used. The chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. ETU (98 %) was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). PTU (analytical standard), cerium sulfate, anhydrous citric acid (CA), l-serine (l-ser), threonine (Thr), EDA, methane sulfonic acid (MSA, HPLC-grade), nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT), and ethylene glycol (EG) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine oxide (TEMP) and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HPLC-grade methanol was purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany).

To prepare stock solutions of ETU and PTU (200 mg‧L−1), we accurately weighed ETU and PTU and dissolved them in methanol. The stock solutions were stored at −18 °C in the dark. Mixed standard solutions of ETU and PTU were diluted with water to the corresponding concentrations and prepared for immediate use. A 0.1 M stock solution of Ce(IV) was obtained by dissolving cerium sulfate in a hot 2 M H2SO4 solution. A 0.4 mM Ce(IV) solution containing 8 mM H2SO4 and 0.04 M HCl was obtained by diluting an appropriate amount of the 0.1 M Ce(IV) solution and conc. HCl with pure water.

The morphology of the as-prepared CDs was characterized using a JEM-2100 microscope (JEOL Co., Akishima, Tokyo, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The FT-IR spectra of the CDs and A-CDs were acquired using a Nicolet iS50 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, Wisconsin, USA). Raman spectra were collected using a Renishaw inVia Raman microscope with a 532 nm laser (Renishaw, Inc., Hoffman Estates, IL, USA). The FL and CL spectra of all CDs were recorded using an F-280 FL spectrophotometer (Tianjin Gangdong Technology Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China). UV–Vis spectra were obtained using a UV-1900 spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co. Ltd., Beijing, China). Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were obtained using an EPR200M electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (CIQTEK, Hefei, China).

The optimized A-CDs were synthesized using EDA, CA, and l-Ser as precursors via a one-pot microwave-assisted pyrolysis process. Briefly, 0.5 g each of CA and l-Ser were added to a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) crucible, dissolved in 2.5 mL of water, and mixed. Subsequently, 80 μL of EDA was added to the crucible and stirred for uniform dispersion. The crucible was then placed in a household microwave oven, heated at 400 W for 9 min, and cooled to 25 °C. The resultant dark brown gelatinous solid was dissolved in ultrapure water and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove insoluble impurities. The resultant dark brown solution was dialyzed (MWCO: 100 Da) for 12 h to eliminate small-molecular-weight materials and byproducts. Finally, the solution was adjusted to 50 mL with pure water and stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator.

Vegetables were purchased from a local agricultural market (Nanchang, China). The surface dirt on the vegetables was removed, and the potatoes, Chinese yams, and sweet potatoes were peeled. Other vegetable samples were collected from edible parts. The samples were chopped using a QSJ-B03H2 household meat grinder (Bear, Shunde, China) and homogenized using an XHF-D high-speed homogenizer (Ningbo Scientz Biotechnology, Ningbo, China). Subsequently, 5.00 ± 0.01 g of the homogenized sample (accurately weighed) was transferred to a 50 mL polyethylene centrifuge tube. The tube was freeze-dried, and 15 mL of HPLC-grade methanol was added to it. The mixture was ultrasonicated for 30 min and filtered through a sand-core funnel. The extraction process was repeated two more times, and the organic extracts were combined. The solvent was concentrated by rotary evaporation at 50 °C, and 5 mL of pure water was added to the residue. The solution was then dissolved through ultrasonication for 3 min and filtered through a 0.22 μm aqueous filter membrane. The processed samples were stored at −18 °C until use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures of HPLC-CL detection with A-CDs as probes
ce:section.ce:para: A schematic of the HPLC-CL device is shown in Fig. 1 . The HPLC separation system consists of an Ecclassical D3200 HPLC system with an automatic sampler (Dalian Elite Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd., Dalian, China) and a SunFire C18 column (4.6 mm × 250 mm, 5 μm, Waters, Ireland). The HPLC parameters were as follows: Mobile phase A: methanol; mobile phase B: water; and mobile phase C: an aqueous solution of 0.01 % MSA. An isocratic elution was performed at a mobile phase A:B:C ratio of 4:76:20. The pump flow rate was 1.0 mL·min−1, the column temperature was maintained at 35 °C, and the sample injection volume was 100 μL.

The CL detection section comprised a BT100-1F peristaltic pump (Longer Precision Pump, Baoding, China), a laboratory-prepared reaction quartz coil (flow cell: 2 mm, i.d.), an RFL-1 chemiluminescence detector (Remex, Xi'an, China), and an RF-A multifunctional chemiluminescence detector (Remex), with all components in the flow system connected using polytetrafluoroethylene (0.5 mm, i.d.). The A-CD solution was first mixed with the chromatographic column effluent through a three-way valve at a flow rate of 3.5 mL‧min−1. Subsequently, the mixture was mixed with an acidic Ce(IV) solution as the oxidant through another three-way valve. The resultant mixture was rapidly flown into the flow cell to produce the CL emission. The CL signal was captured using a photomultiplier tube (PMT) located below the flow cell and recorded using a computer equipped with a data-acquisition interface. The relative peak height of the CL intensity (ΔI = I s-I o) was used to quantify ETU and PTU, where I
s and I
o are the maximum CL values of the analyte and baseline CL signal, respectively.

Each sample was analyzed in triplicate, and the average recoveries and relative standard deviations (RSDs) were calculated. The results were presented as mean ± standard deviation. Data were analyzed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by Duncan's test, where the values were considered statistically significant for p < 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and characterization of chemiluminescent quantum dot probes
ce:section.ce:para: A-CDs serve as crucial luminescent agents in the CL system. The structure and surface functional groups of A-CDs influence their interactions with Ce(IV) atoms, thereby determining the enhancement effect of ETU or PTU between A-CDs and Ce(IV) atoms. These surface functional groups originate from the precursors. Therefore, detailed experiments were conducted to assess the influence of the precursors, mass ratio of the precursors, preparation method, microwave power, and preparation time on the CL produced by ETU or PTU of the same concentration.

A series of CDs with various precursors were synthesized via microwave, solid-state, and hydrothermal methods, and their influence on the CL signals from ETU–Ce(IV) and PTU–Ce(IV) was investigated. All CDs except those prepared from CA did not enhance the CL signals. CDs prepared from CA and amino acids without β-hydroxyl groups showed suboptimal CL enhancement under identical conditions. Augmented ETU–Ce(IV) and PTU–Ce(IV) CL signals were observed only for CDs prepared from l-Ser and CA. The l-Ser-based CDs showed higher CL enhancement compared to Thr-based CDs. This could be attributed to the steric hindrance of the β-hydroxyl group of Thr, a secondary alcohol, whereas l-Ser is a primary alcohol.

To investigate the effect of different preparation methods for CDs on their resultant CL, CDs with a CA:l-Ser mass ratio of 1:1 were obtained by microwave-assisted pyrolysis, solid-state pyrolysis, and the hydrothermal method. ETU and PTU showed the strongest enhancement on the CL of the Ce(IV) –CDs system synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis (Fig. 2a). This is attributed to the shorter synthesis time of microwave-assisted pyrolysis than those of solid-state pyrolysis and the hydrothermal method, which preserved the abundant active groups on the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mass ratios of the CD precursors
ce:section.ce:para: The mass ratio of the two CD precursors (CA and l-Ser) affects the structure and surface functional groups of the resultant CDs. To optimize the ratio, a series of CDs with CA:l-Ser mass ratios of 2:1, 1.5:1, 1:1, 1:1.5, and 1:2 was prepared while maintaining a constant total mass (1 g each). The CDs with a 1:1 CA:l-Ser mass ratio produced the strongest CL at the same ETU and PTU concentrations (Fig. 2b). As the mass ratio decreased from 2:1 to 1:1, a higher l-Ser content increased the CL nearly linearly. However, when the CA ratio was less than 50 %, only molten CA and l-Ser remained in the microwave because of the lower melting point of CA compared to that of l-Ser. In this case, CA serves as a precursor and a co-solvent. Hence, as the proportion of CA decreased in the mixture, the temperature of the molten mixture increased, exacerbating the carbonization of the precursor. Consequently, the surface group composition of CDs significantly changed, decreasing the CL of ETU–Ce(IV) and PTU–Ce(IV).

The effect of the microwave power (400, 600, and 800 W) on the CL of ETU–Ce(IV) and PTU–Ce(IV) was investigated at a CA:l-Ser mass ratio of 1:1 and a synthesis time of 8 min. The CDs synthesized at 400 W exhibited the strongest CL emission (Fig. 2c). However, extremely high microwave power can lead to high precursor center temperatures, damaging the surface functional groups of the CDs. Hence, 400 W was selected as the optimal microwave power for fabricating CDs in the subsequent experiments.

Even though CDs synthesized from the two aforementioned precursors and Ce(IV) can generate CL emission with ETU and PTU catalysis, their sensitivity as CL probes for detecting ETU and PTU does not meet the requirements. To achieve the highly sensitive detection of target analytes, further improvements in CDs are required. Doping CDs with electron-donating elements, such as N, O, S, and P, can enhance their activity(Yang et al., 2023). N is the most frequently used element for doping and can reduce the work function of CDs by injecting electrons or reducing the bandgaps and emission energies of CDs(Mathew & Mathew, 2023). When coupling agents such as EDA and EG were used during CD fabrication, stronger CL emissions were observed in the presence of EDA. Subsequently, the effect of the EDA volume (0–80 μL) on CL was investigated. The presence of EDA enhanced both the signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio and the CL signals of CD-ETU–Ce(IV) and CD-PTU–Ce(IV) (Fig. 2d). However, when the EDA volume was above 80 μL, CL significantly decreased. EDA plays a role of coupling agents in the preparation of A-CDs. When the volume of EDA beyond 80 μL, the number of amide group on the surface of A-CDs will increase and polymerization of A-CDs enhance. Furthermore, the hydrophilic effect of A-CDs will descent. As a result, the CL intensity slides. Overall, 80 μL was selected as the optimal EDA volume for the preparation of A-CDs in subsequent experiments.

We investigated the effect of the microwave duration (5–10 min) on the CL of the fabricated CDs. The maximum S/N ratio was observed at a microwave duration of 9 min (Fig. 2e) in the presence of ETU–Ce(IV) and PTU–Ce(IV). However, beyond 9 min, the produced A-CDs aggregated owing to carbonization, which decreased the S/N ratio. Hence, 9 min was selected as the optimal microwave synthesis time for A-CDs.

The microstructures of the CDs were characterized using TEM (Fig. 3a). The CDs synthesized from CA and l-Ser were uniformly dispersed and spherical, with an average particle size of 3.33 ± 0.54 nm and a particle size distribution of 2.0–4.5 nm. The CDs remained spherical and uniformly distributed even after the addition of EDA; the resultant particles had an average particle size of 4.70 ± 0.98 nm and a particle size distribution of 2.0–7.0 nm, indicating a significant increase compared to those of the CDs prepared without EDA. This can be attributed to the formation of amide bonds between the amino groups in the EDA molecules and the carboxyl groups in the CA, which accelerates the polymerization of the precursors and increases the particle size of the CDs. Thus, the CDs with EDA as additives were named A-CDs. The quantum yields of the two types of CDs were calculated to be 0.22 and 0.21, respectively. The quantum yields of the A-CDs decreased slightly after amidation.

The XRD patterns of the A-CDs are shown in Fig. 3b. The A-CDs exhibit a broad, camel-shaped peak at 20.30°. This suggests that a typical amorphous carbon structure was produced from microwave pyrolysis. Notably, this peak is to the left of the graphene peak reported in the literature(Liu et al., 2020). This peak indicates the lower graphitization degree in A-CDs, along with the presence of N and O impurities. This could be attributed to a greater interlayer spacing in carbon in the A-CDs.(Zhang et al., 2021).

The surface structures of the synthesized CDs were characterized and compared using FT-IR (Fig. 3c). Both types of CDs showed a broad absorption peak at 3600–2500 cm−1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH groups in polycarboxylates. Moreover, the absorption peaks at 1720, 1597, 1408, and 1208 cm−1 in the spectra of both CDs correspond to CO, CC, CN, and CO stretching vibrations, respectively. Upon the addition of EDA, the peak intensities of the stretching vibrations of CN, O=C–NH, and C–O(H) (1687, 1380, and 1091 cm−1, respectively) significantly increased. This indicates the formation of more CN and amide bonds between EDA and L-ser, which stabilize the carbon skeleton structure of the CDs. This conclusion is consistent with the Raman spectral results.

Fig. 3d shows a survey XPS profile of the A-CDs. O1s, N1s, and C1s peaks are observed at 531, 399, and 286 eV, respectively, and the atomic percentages of C, N, and O are 58.72 %, 11.92 %, and 29.36 %, respectively. The N atomic percentage of the A-CDs in the presence of EDA (11.92 %) is higher than that without EDA (9.26 %). This is consistent with the FT-IR spectral results. The high-resolution C1s, N1s, and O1s XPS profiles of the A-CDs are shown in Figs. 3f–h. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 3e) was deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.3, 285.5, 287.4, and 289.6 eV, which are attributed to the binding energies of C in C–C/C=C, C–N/C–O, C=N/C=O, and O=C–O(H), respectively(Bai et al., 2020). The high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 3f) was deconvoluted into three peaks at 398.4, 399.9, and 401.2 eV, which are attributed to the binding energies of N in pyridine-N, pyrrole-N, and graphitic-N, respectively(Wei, Cai, et al., 2024; Ye et al., 2020). The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 3g) was deconvoluted into three peaks corresponding to the binding energies of O in CO (530.9 eV), CO (532.4 eV), and O=C–O(H) (533.5 eV)(Fu et al., 2023). Overall, the FT-IR and XPS profiles confirmed a higher N content in the A-CDs prepared with EDA than those without EDA and the presence of hydrophilic groups such as carboxyl, amide, and hydroxyl groups on the A-CD surfaces in the presence of EDA(Wei, Gan, et al., 2024).

The Raman spectra of the CDs (Fig. 3h) revealed D and G bands at 1365 and 1578 cm−1, respectively. A lower intensity ratio of the D and G bands (ID/IG) suggests a higher degree of graphitization(Mathew & Mathew, 2023). The ID/IG of the CDs synthesized from CA and l-Ser was 0.84, which decreased to 0.77 upon the addition of EDA during CD synthesis from CA and l-Ser. Hence, under identical synthesis conditions, EDA can enhance the graphitization of CDs. In addition, in the presence of EDA, graphitic-N peak in XPS was clearly detected. On the other hand, no graphitic-N peak was found without EDA(Wei, Gan, et al., 2024). The increment of graphitization degree could stabilize and prolong the lifetime of excited CL emitters. Consequently, A-CDs with the addition of EDA enhance intensity of the present CL system.

Fig. 3i shows the UV–Vis, excitation, and emission FL spectra of the A-CDs. The UV–Vis spectra of the A-CDs exhibit three absorption peaks at 207, 240, and 380 nm. The absorption peaks at 207 and 240 nm, corresponding to the π → π* transitions in the sp2 domain(Hu et al., 2021), are characteristic of CDs. The peak at 380 nm corresponds to the n → π* transitions of heteroatoms (N and O)(Lamba et al., 2024). Moreover, the maximum FL emission and excitation wavelengths of the A-CDs were 447 and 377 nm, respectively. The excitation and FL spectra demonstrated good symmetry, and the maximum excitation wavelength and UV–Vis absorbance were at ∼380 nm. The 1:2500 diluted A-CD aqueous solution was colorless and transparent under sunlight but was bright blue when illuminated with UV light (365 nm; inset of Fig. 3i).

The maximum FL intensity of the A-CDs initially increased and then decreased in the range of 300–430 nm (Fig. 3j). However, as the excitation wavelength increased, the maximum FL wavelength was significantly red-shifted. This could be attributed to the changes in the numerous energy gaps of the diverse functional groups on the CD surfaces(Yan et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the quantitative CL detection of ETU and its homologs

ce:section.ce:para: A CL kinetic curve demonstrates the formation of the CL emitters and energy release patterns. A mixture of an acidic Ce(IV) solution and ETU or PTU did not produce a CL signal. However, a solution of Ce(IV) and A-CDs (Fig. S1a) produced a weak CL signal, wherein the CL intensity was initially high. A solution of Ce(IV), PTU or ETU, and A-CDs produced a strong CL signal (Fig. S1b and Fig. S1c). The similarity of the three kinetic curves suggests that ETU or PTU catalyzes the reaction between the A-CDs and Ce (IV), producing an intense CL emission.

First, the CL spectra were acquired using an FL spectrophotometer without excitation. All three CL spectra exhibited identical maximum emission peaks at ∼530 nm (Fig. 4a), albeit with varying intensities, suggesting the involvement of the same luminescent species. The CL emission intensity significantly increased in the presence of ETU or PTU, and the CL intensities did not differ at the same ETU and PTU concentrations. In addition, a weak emission peak at 380 nm could be observed with the same intensity, which is attributed to Ce(III)⁎. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of the A-CDs before the reaction is marked by the purple line (line d) in Fig. 4a. At an excitation wavelength of 435 nm, the maximum PL emission wavelength of the A-CDs was 477 nm. After the reaction, the maximum PL wavelength of the oxidized A-CDs (Ox-A-CDs) was 510 nm (line e), which indicates a pronounced red shift. Moreover, the PL intensity of the Ox-A-CDs was lower than that of the initial A-CDs. Oxidized CDs typically have higher surface defects(Yan et al., 2019), which could explain the red shift. Overall, the CL and FL spectra indicate that the primary luminescent species in this CL system are Ox-A-CDs⁎.

The UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 4b) showed maximum absorption peaks of PTU (line a) and Ce(IV) (line f) at 233 and 310 nm, respectively. The UV–Vis spectrum of a mixture of Ce(IV) and PTU (line e) showed a lower Ce(IV) peak intensity owing to the reaction of Ce(IV) with PTU. Similarly, the spectrum of a mixture of Ce(IV) and A-CDs (line d) exhibited a lower Ce(IV) peak intensity owing to the consumption of more Ce(IV) ions by A-CDs. The UV–Vis peak of the ternary system of PTU, A-CDs, and Ce(IV) disappeared, indicating that A-CDs promoted the oxidation reaction of Ce(IV) on PTU.

TEMP, DMPO, and NBT were used to capture the reactive oxygen species (ROS) formed in the A-CD–Ce(IV) and A-CD–ETU–Ce(IV) CL systems (Fig. S2). Free radical trapping agents such as ascorbic acid, sodium azide, and p-benzoquinone were not used because they can react with Ce(IV), consume oxidants, and quench the CL signals. NBT is commonly used to scavenge superoxide anions (O2 ·−)(Heller & Croot, 2010). The addition of 1 mM NBT did not suppress the CL emissions of the A-CDs–Ce(IV) CL system but suppressed the CL signal of the A-CD–ETU–Ce(IV) CL system by 30.83 %. This indicates that the O2 ·− species are produced only in the A-CDs–ETU–Ce(IV) CL system. This also suggests that both systems have different CL mechanisms. TEMP scavenges the 1O2 species(Hidegl et al., 1994). The addition of 1 mM TEMP enhanced the CL signal of the A-CD–Ce (IV) system by 36.31 % but suppressed that of the A-CD–ETU–Ce(IV) system by 37.34 %. DMPO is a scavenger for hydroxyl radicals such as •OH and O2 •−.(Chen et al., 2022) The addition of DMPO suppressed the CL signals of both systems (not shown in Fig. S2), indicating the presence of •OH groups. Thiourea is an •OH scavenger. The addition of 1 mM thiourea(Wasil et al., 1987) which is considered a high concentration, suppressed the CL of the A-CDs–Ce(IV) system significantly; in contrast, low concentrations of thiourea enhanced the CL of this system. These results seem contradictory. Virtually, when the concentration of thiourea is high, all Ce(IV) are completely consumed by thiourea and could not react with A-CDs to generate CL emission. Additionally, we used an EPR spectrometer to identify the three types of ROS. Unfortunately, all these ROS species like O2 •−, •OH and 1O2 were not detected.

Both ETU and PTU are small sulfur-containing molecules. We examined whether they were oxidized by Ce(IV) to SO2 ⁎, which is then transferred to the A-CDs via chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (CRET) to produce the CL emission. Quinine sulfate can accept energy from SO2 ⁎ via CRET, producing CL emission(Asghar et al., 2019; Asghar et al., 2022). However, the addition of 1 μM quinine sulfate to the ETU–Ce(IV) and PTU–Ce(IV) CL systems did not produce CL emission. This indicates that the CL mechanism of our system does not involve the CRET of SO2 ⁎.

Subsequently, the reaction products of PTU and Ce(IV) were analyzed using an AB Sciex API 4000 plus mass spectrometer (Fig. S3). The m/z peak of 101.1 indicates the formation of 4-methyl-2-imidazolidine from the desulfurization of PTU by Ce(IV).

The mechanisms of CL from the reaction of the A-CDs and Ce(IV) catalyzed by PTU and ETU are shown in Fig. 5 . The A-CDs are oxidized by Ce(IV) to form Ox-A-CDs⁎, which emit weak CL (λmax = 530 nm). Concurrently, Ce(IV) is reduced to Ce(III)⁎, which emits CL (λmax = 380 nm). In contrast, ETU or PTU are oxidized by Ce(IV), wherein the CS bond loses an electron, generating S-based free radicals. These free radicals donate electrons to A-CDs, producing A-CDs·−. Moreover, Ce(IV) introduces holes into the A-CDs, forming A-CDs·+. Both A-CDs·− and A-CDs·+ produce Ox-A-CDs⁎, which emit CL (λmax = 530 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the HPLC-CL method for ETU and PTU quantification

ce:section.ce:para: ETU and PTU are strongly polar imidazole-like compounds and are soluble in water. Therefore, they must be separated via the HPLC method for simultaneous quantification. Their imidazole rings may likely form hydrogen bonds with the residual silanol groups in the stationary phase, which could lead to peak tailing. Hence, we added 0.002 % (v/v) MSA to the HPLC mobile phase based on our previous literature(Lai et al., 2020). Although a mobile phase with a high concentration of an organic solvent can separate the two analytes in a standard solution, it produces interference in the actual samples. Therefore, we used 4 % methanol or acetonitrile in water as the mobile phase, which successfully separated up to 50 μg·L−1 of ETU and PTU. However, the use of 4 % acetonitrile resulted in a shorter retention time, weaker CL response, and greater noise, resulting in lower sensitivity. The results are shown in Fig. S4.

Previous studies have shown that CL parameters such as the concentration of A-CDs, concentration of the Ce(IV) oxidant, acidic medium and its concentration, and flow rate influence the sensitivity of the HPLC-CL method. Hence, we determined the optimal CL parameters for the highly sensitive quantification of ETU and PTU via the HPLC-CL method.

The A-CD concentration affects the sensitivity and limit of detection (LOD) of the HPLC-CL method. We investigated the effect of different dilution ratios of the A-CD solutions on the S/N ratio of the CL. At higher dilutions of A-CDs, the reaction between Ce(IV) and the A-CDs produced a stronger CL with greater background and noise (Fig. S5a). At a dilution of 1/2500, the S/N ratios of both ETU and PTU reached maximal. Subsequently, as the dilution increased, the S/N ratio decreased. This could be attributed to the low A-CD concentrations. Hence, we selected 1/2500 as the optimal dilution ratio.

The concentration of Ce(IV), the sole oxidant in this CL system, affects the redox reaction rate. We investigated the effect of different concentrations of Ce(IV) on the S/N ratio of the CL. Both target analytes (ETU and PTU) showed similar results (Fig. S5b). As the Ce(IV) concentration was increased from 0.2 to 0.4 mM, the noise remained constant while the CL signal increased; in other words, the S/N ratio of the two analytes increased. Beyond 0.4 mM Ce(IV), the baseline noise increased while the CL signal did not increase; this reduces the S/N ratio. Therefore, the optimal Ce(IV) concentration was 0.4 mM.

An acidic environment increases the oxidation capability of Ce(IV). Hence, we screened 0.05 M solutions of HAc, MSA, HNO3, HClO4, HCl, and H2SO4 for the preparation of a 0.4 mM Ce (IV) solution by observing their effects on the ETU(PTU)–A-CDs–Ce(IV) CL system (Fig. S5c). The Ce(IV) solution containing HCl produced the strongest CL and highest S/N ratio, followed by that containing HNO3. Subsequently, we investigated the optimal HCl concentration for low S/N ratios of ETU and PTU (Fig. S5d). The CL and S/N ratio both increased as the HCl concentration increased from 0.02 to 0.04 M and then decreased beyond 0.04 M. Hence, 0.04 M HCl was selected as the optimal acidic medium.

The flow rate determines the mixing speed of the CL reagents and consequently affects the timing of the maximum CL emission. We investigated the effects of flow rates from 1.0 to 4.5 mL·min−1 on the S/N ratio of PTU and ETU (Fig. S5e). Higher flow rates produced a higher CL and S/N ratio. The CL process is a rapid reaction, reaching its peak in less than 1 s, and a higher flow rate enhances the CL and S/N ratio (Fig. 4). Moreover, the methanol in the mobile phase inhibits CL emissions. A higher flow rate reduces this inhibitory effect. A flow rate beyond 3.5 mL·min−1 dilutes ETU (or PTU) with the CL reagent and postpones the maximum luminescence beyond the detection window, thereby reducing the S/N ratio. Hence, we selected 3.5 mL·min−1 as the peristaltic pump flow rate.

This method was validated according to the 2002/657/EC Decision(European Commission, 2002). Under optimized experimental conditions, the standard chromatograms of ETU and PTU (both 80 μg·L−1) under the aforementioned optimal CL detection conditions were acquired (Fig. S5). The retention times of ETU and PTU were 4.97 min and 8.83 min, respectively. Hence, this method successfully separated and detected the two analytes within 10 min. The chromatographic peaks were symmetric, and the retention times were reproducible. Moreover, a stable CL baseline and minimal background noise were observed. Overall, this analytical method was highly sensitive.

Standard curves were developed using triplicate measurements of the mixed standard working solutions at different concentrations (Fig. S6). The standard curves, linear ranges, and LODs of ETU and PTU are listed in Table S1. The LODs (S/N = 3) of ETU and PTU are 0.17 and 0.23 μg‧kg−1, respectively.

Table 1 compares the proposed HPLC-CL method with previously reported analytical methods, including HPLC-DAD(López-Fernández et al., 2014), UPLC-MS/MS(Bonnechère et al., 2011), HPLC-ICP-MS(Bendhiab et al., 2023), GC–MS(López-Fernández et al., 2014), and UHPLC-ECD(Nantaphol et al., 2020). The LOD of our method is the lowest among those of the previously reported methods. Moreover, this method does not require purification procedures, and is less time- and labor-intensive.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the HPLC-CL method to real samples

ce:section.ce:para: In this study, we fabricated CL probes (A-CDs) for the real-time, simultaneous, and quantitative detection of the carcinogen ETU and its homologs. A plausible CL mechanism was proposed. These probes rapidly captured electrons from sulfur-containing free radicals and annihilated them with oxidized A-CDs (Ox-A-CDs‧+) to generate CL. The A-CD probes were integrated into a HPLC-CL method, which was validated by quantifying ETU and PTU in real vegetable samples. Our method involved the crude methanol extraction of the test samples and thus eliminates complex purification steps. Moreover, our method was selective and highly sensitive. Thus, our HPLC-CL method can be used to separate thiourea pollutants and can promote the design of novel carbon nanomaterials and their chemiluminescent applications.

Guowen Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Xianglei Cheng: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
